title: Enums, Pattern Matching & Option — Quick Check

questions.q: Why must a `match` expression in Rust cover all variants of an enum?
questions.a: Rust enforces exhaustive matching at compile time. If any variant is unhandled the code will not compile, making it impossible to ship code that silently ignores a case. This eliminates a whole class of runtime bugs common in switch-based languages.

questions.q: What is `Option<T>` and what problem does it solve compared to null in other languages?
questions.a: `Option<T>` is an enum with two variants: `Some(T)` when a value is present, `None` when absent. Unlike null, the compiler forces you to handle both variants before accessing the inner value — eliminating null pointer exceptions entirely.

questions.q: What is the difference between `if let` and `match`? When would you prefer each?
questions.a: `if let` is syntactic sugar for a match with one handled arm and an implicit `_ => ()` fallthrough. Use `if let` when you only care about one pattern; use `match` when you need to handle multiple variants, especially for exhaustiveness.

questions.q: How do you extract data from an enum variant that carries a value? Write a one-line example.
questions.a: Use pattern matching to destructure: `if let Some(value) = my_option { ... }` or inside a match arm: `Message::Write(text) => println!("{text}")`. You cannot access variant data directly like a struct field.
